description: Manually curated cross-disciplinary paper pairs that share a deep conceptual idea but come from different fields. Used to evaluate whether the GNN finds connections that citation structure and arXiv categories miss. The model is never trained on these pairs — they are used only for evaluation.
evaluation_protocol: For each pair, compute the cosine similarity rank of side B among all candidate papers when querying with the embedding of side A, and vice versa. Report mean reciprocal rank (MRR) and recall@10, recall@50, recall@100 across all pairs.

pairs.label: Langevin dynamics → Diffusion models
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Langevin dynamics: a stochastic differential equation describing the motion of a particle subject to a potential gradient and random thermal noise. Used in statistical mechanics to model diffusion processes and sample from Boltzmann distributions via overdamped Langevin MCMC.
pairs.ml_side: Denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPM): generative models that learn to reverse a fixed Markov noising process. The reverse process is parameterized as a neural network that estimates the score (gradient of the log data density), equivalent to the drift term in Langevin dynamics.
pairs.shared_idea: Both describe a system that adds noise according to a known process and then recovers structure by following the gradient of a log-probability (score function). Score-based diffusion models are a direct mathematical generalization of annealed Langevin MCMC sampling to learned data distributions.
pairs.notes: The connection is mathematically precise: Song & Ermon (2019) explicitly derive score-based models from Langevin dynamics. However, the physics literature predates the ML literature by decades and uses completely different terminology. SPECTER2 should partially capture this via co-citation with the Song & Ermon paper, but older physics papers will not cite it.

pairs.label: Variational Bayes → Variational Autoencoders
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Variational Bayes (VB): a family of approximate inference methods that recast posterior computation as optimization. Introduces a variational distribution Q and minimizes KL(Q || P) with respect to Q, yielding the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO). Developed in the machine learning and statistics communities in the 1990s-2000s.
pairs.ml_side: Variational Autoencoders (VAE): deep generative models (Kingma & Welling, 2014) that combine variational inference with neural network amortization. The encoder outputs variational parameters; the reparameterization trick enables backpropagation through stochastic latent variables. The objective is the ELBO.
pairs.shared_idea: VAEs are a direct instantiation of variational Bayes where the variational family is parameterized by a neural network (amortized inference). The ELBO objective, the KL divergence term, and the reconstruction term map directly. The reparameterization trick is the key technical innovation that makes the VB framework differentiable with respect to encoder parameters.
pairs.notes: This pair is partially findable via direct citation (VAE papers cite Blei et al. 2017 and Jordan et al. 1999), so SPECTER2 may already rank these close. The harder version is: can the model find older VB papers from before the VAE era that the VAE papers do not cite?

pairs.label: Renormalization group → Multiscale graph methods
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Renormalization group (RG): a systematic framework for studying how a physical system's description changes as one zooms out and integrates out short-range degrees of freedom. Used in statistical mechanics, quantum field theory, and condensed matter. Key operations: block-spin coarsening, decimation, fixed-point analysis.
pairs.ml_side: Hierarchical and multiscale graph methods: graph pooling, graph coarsening, and hierarchical GNNs (DiffPool, MinCut pooling, etc.) that progressively compress a graph while preserving its large-scale structure. Also includes graph wavelets and spectral multiscale methods.
pairs.shared_idea: Both describe a process of iterative coarsening: merging fine-grained units into effective coarse-grained representations while preserving the essential information at each scale. The fixed point of RG corresponds to the limit of infinite graph depth in hierarchical GNNs. Some papers (e.g. Beny & Osborne on RG and information geometry) make this connection explicit.
pairs.notes: This pair is conceptually strong but terminologically distant. Few papers explicitly bridge both literatures. The model must discover the connection from structural patterns in the graph, not from shared vocabulary. Mehta & Schwab (2014) are a key bridging paper that cites both sides.

pairs.label: Belief propagation → Graph neural message passing
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Belief propagation (BP): an algorithm for exact or approximate inference on graphical models (Bayesian networks, Markov random fields) that works by iteratively passing local messages between neighboring nodes. Sum-product algorithm for marginals; max-product for MAP inference. Used in error-correcting codes, computer vision, and physics (Bethe approximation).
pairs.ml_side: Message passing neural networks (MPNN): the general framework for GNNs (Gilmer et al. 2017) where each node aggregates messages from its neighbors and updates its state. This is a direct neural generalization of belief propagation, replacing fixed message functions with learned ones.
pairs.shared_idea: Message passing neural networks are an explicit neural generalization of belief propagation. The 'messages' in MPNN correspond directly to BP messages; the 'aggregation' step corresponds to the product (or sum) over incoming messages; the 'update' corresponds to the marginal computation. Gilmer et al. acknowledge this connection explicitly.
pairs.notes: This connection is well-known within the GNN community and is explicitly cited in many GNN papers. The challenge is finding early BP papers from the 1990s-2000s that predated the GNN literature and were not themselves aware of the connection. Those early papers will not appear in the GNN citation graph.

pairs.label: Ising model → Energy-based models
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Ising model: a canonical model in statistical mechanics defining a probability distribution over binary spin configurations via a Boltzmann energy function. Partition function is the normalizing constant. Exact inference is #P-hard on general graphs; tractable on chains and planar graphs. Gibbs sampling provides MCMC inference.
pairs.ml_side: Energy-based models (EBMs): a family of generative models (including Boltzmann machines, deep Boltzmann machines, and modern neural EBMs) that define an unnormalized probability distribution via a learned energy function E(x). Training involves contrastive divergence, MCMC, or score matching to estimate the gradient of the log-partition function.
pairs.shared_idea: Energy-based models in ML are a direct generalization of the Ising/Potts models from statistical mechanics to continuous and high-dimensional inputs with learned (neural) energy functions. The partition function intractability, MCMC inference, and the Gibbs sampling inference procedure are identical in both. Restricted Boltzmann Machines are literally a bipartite Ising model.
pairs.notes: Boltzmann machines directly bridge both fields, so papers about RBMs or DBMs will appear in both citation graphs. The harder pairs are: modern neural EBM papers that do not cite the physics literature, and classic statistical mechanics papers that predated the ML connection.

pairs.label: Principal component analysis → Autoencoders
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Principal component analysis (PCA): projects data onto the directions of maximum variance (principal components), which are the leading eigenvectors of the covariance matrix. Equivalent to SVD of the centered data matrix. Provides the optimal linear reconstruction under squared error. Widely used in statistics, signal processing, and neuroscience.
pairs.ml_side: Autoencoders (AE): neural networks trained to reconstruct their input via a bottleneck representation. Linear autoencoders with squared loss and orthonormal weights provably recover the PCA subspace. Nonlinear autoencoders (with sigmoid, ReLU, etc.) generalize PCA to nonlinear manifolds.
pairs.shared_idea: Linear autoencoders are provably equivalent to PCA: the encoder learns the PCA projection and the decoder learns the reconstruction. Nonlinear autoencoders generalize PCA by learning a nonlinear manifold structure. This connection motivates the use of autoencoders as a data-driven replacement for PCA in settings where the data distribution is non-Gaussian.
pairs.notes: The PCA-AE connection is well-known and frequently cited in tutorial papers. The challenge is finding early PCA-focused papers from statistics journals (where they don't use ML terminology) that are conceptually equivalent to what ML papers call autoencoders.

pairs.label: Optimal transport → Wasserstein GAN
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Optimal transport (OT): a mathematical framework for computing the minimum-cost way to transform one probability measure into another (the Wasserstein distance, also called Earth Mover's distance). The Kantorovich formulation is a linear program; the Sinkhorn algorithm provides efficient entropic regularized approximations.
pairs.ml_side: Wasserstein GAN (WGAN): a variant of GANs that replaces the Jensen-Shannon divergence loss with the Wasserstein-1 distance between real and generated distributions. The critic (formerly discriminator) is constrained to be 1-Lipschitz to provide a tractable estimate of the Wasserstein distance via the Kantorovich-Rubinstein duality.
pairs.shared_idea: WGAN directly instantiates optimal transport theory in the GAN framework. The Wasserstein distance is a well-studied OT distance; the Kantorovich-Rubinstein duality is the mathematical foundation for the WGAN critic objective. The Lipschitz constraint on the critic is the operational form of the dual problem's constraint.
pairs.notes: The Arjovsky et al. WGAN paper (2017) explicitly cites the OT literature, so this pair has a direct citation link. The challenge is finding earlier OT papers (Villani's textbook, Rubner et al. 1998) that have no citation link to GAN papers but establish the same theoretical foundation.

pairs.label: Kalman filter → Recurrent networks (LSTM)
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Kalman filter: an optimal linear estimator for the state of a discrete-time linear dynamical system with Gaussian noise. Uses a prediction step (prior state estimate) and an update step (posterior after observing measurement), equivalent to Bayesian filtering on a linear Gaussian state space model. Extended Kalman filter handles nonlinear dynamics.
pairs.ml_side: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks: recurrent neural networks with gated memory cells that address the vanishing gradient problem. The cell state update and gate structure can be interpreted as a learned nonlinear generalization of the Kalman filter update equations.
pairs.shared_idea: Both maintain a running state estimate that is updated by new observations and decayed or gated by a learned or fixed transition model. The Kalman gain corresponds to the LSTM input gate; the prediction step corresponds to the forget gate × previous cell state. This connection is formalized in papers on recurrent Kalman networks and deep state space models.
pairs.notes: Recurrent Kalman networks and deep SSMs (Rangapuram et al. 2018, Fraccaro et al. 2017) are explicit bridging papers that cite both sides. The challenge is connecting early Kalman filter papers from the 1960s control theory literature with modern LSTM papers — these have no citation path.

pairs.label: Random matrix theory → Deep neural network initialization / generalization
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Random matrix theory (RMT): the study of the spectral properties of large matrices with random entries. Key results: Marchenko-Pastur law (eigenvalue distribution of Wishart matrices), semicircle law (Wigner matrices), free probability. Used in nuclear physics, wireless communications, and portfolio theory.
pairs.ml_side: Deep learning theory using RMT: analysis of weight matrix spectra in trained neural networks. RMT predicts the bulk eigenvalue distribution of random initialization; deviations from the bulk in trained networks indicate learned structure. Also used to analyze the Hessian of the loss landscape and understand generalization.
pairs.shared_idea: The spectral bulk of large random weight matrices follows RMT predictions (Marchenko-Pastur). This provides a theoretical baseline for 'what random looks like' and allows identification of which singular values/vectors carry learned information. Papers on optimal layer-wise learning rates, weight pruning, and implicit regularization use RMT as the analytical framework.
pairs.notes: Martin & Mahoney's work on heavy-tailed self-regularization explicitly bridges both fields. The connection is real but the RMT literature does not know about neural networks, making this a genuinely hard cross-disciplinary pair for automatic discovery.

pairs.label: Hamilton-Jacobi equations → Reinforcement learning (HJB / optimal control)
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation: a PDE characterizing the optimal value function in continuous-time stochastic optimal control. The solution gives the optimal policy via its gradient. Pontryagin's maximum principle is the ODE analog for deterministic control.
pairs.ml_side: Reinforcement learning (RL): model-free and model-based methods for learning optimal policies via interaction with an environment. Value-based RL (Q-learning, DQN) learns a discrete-time approximation to the HJB equation. Policy gradient methods implicitly optimize the same objective through trajectory sampling.
pairs.shared_idea: RL is a sample-based, function-approximation approach to solving the HJB equation. The Bellman equation in RL is the discrete-time version of HJB; Q-learning converges to the optimal Q-function which solves the Bellman optimality equation — a discretized HJB. Deep RL replaces the PDE solver with a neural network.
pairs.notes: The connection between HJB and RL is well-known in the control theory community but less known in the ML community. Early optimal control papers from math/engineering journals will not cite RL papers.

pairs.label: Percolation theory → Dropout regularization
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Percolation theory: the study of connectivity in random graphs or lattices where each edge or node is independently present with probability p. Phase transitions occur at a critical threshold p_c above which a giant connected component emerges. Used to model flow through porous media, epidemic spreading, and network resilience.
pairs.ml_side: Dropout: a regularization technique (Srivastava et al. 2014) that randomly sets each neuron's activation to zero with probability p during training. Prevents co-adaptation of neurons. Can be interpreted as training an exponential ensemble of thinned networks, or as injecting Bernoulli noise into the forward pass.
pairs.shared_idea: Dropout applies Bernoulli random removal (percolation) to the neural network graph. The behavior of signal propagation through a dropped-out network — which information paths survive, what is the effective conductance — is a percolation problem on the computational graph. The phase transition in percolation has an analog in the depth-wise signal propagation theory of deep networks.
pairs.notes: This connection is mostly implicit — few papers explicitly frame dropout as percolation. The similarity is structural (random edge removal) rather than derivational. Useful for testing whether the model finds structural analogies without explicit citation links.

pairs.label: Mean field theory → Batch normalization / layer normalization
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Mean field theory (MFT): an approximation technique in statistical physics that replaces interactions between particles with an effective average 'mean field'. Each particle experiences the average effect of all others, decoupling the many-body problem into independent single-body problems. Widely used in spin models, neural mean field dynamics.
pairs.ml_side: Mean field theory of deep learning: analytical framework (Poole et al. 2016, Schoenholz et al. 2017) that studies signal propagation in deep randomly initialized networks by applying MFT to the forward pass. Predicts the ordered/chaotic phase transition (edge of chaos), correlates with depth of gradient propagation, and motivated batch normalization design.
pairs.shared_idea: Deep network initialization analysis uses MFT: each neuron's pre-activation is approximated by its mean and variance (mean field approximation). Batch normalization enforces the mean field approximation explicitly at each layer by normalizing activations. The ordered-chaotic phase boundary from MFT analysis determines optimal initialization scales.
pairs.notes: Papers by Poole, Ganguli, Saxe explicitly use the term 'mean field theory' and bridge both sides. Earlier physics MFT papers will not know about batch norm.

pairs.label: Information geometry → Natural gradient descent
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Information geometry: the study of statistical manifolds — spaces of probability distributions equipped with the Fisher information metric (a Riemannian metric). The Fisher-Rao metric is the unique metric invariant under sufficient statistics. Geodesics on statistical manifolds are the paths of least 'information distance'.
pairs.ml_side: Natural gradient descent (Amari, 1998): gradient descent in the parameter space of a model, corrected by the inverse Fisher information matrix to account for the curvature of the statistical manifold. Equivalent to Newton's method in distribution space. Used in RL (TRPO, NPG) and variational inference (natural gradient VI).
pairs.shared_idea: Natural gradient descent is an application of information geometry to optimization: instead of gradient descent in Euclidean parameter space, it follows the steepest descent on the Fisher-Rao statistical manifold. The Fisher information matrix is the Riemannian metric. TRPO and related policy optimization methods are practical approximations to natural gradient steps.
pairs.notes: Amari's natural gradient paper is the direct bridge. But the differential geometry literature on statistical manifolds (e.g. Amari & Nagaoka's textbook, Chentsov's work) predates the RL connection by decades and uses entirely different vocabulary.

pairs.label: Spike timing in neuroscience → Attention mechanisms
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Spike-timing dependent plasticity (STDP) and attentional gating in neuroscience: biological neural networks exhibit selective attention — neurons in higher cortical areas can selectively amplify signals from specific input neurons depending on task context. Top-down attention modulates bottom-up sensory signals via gain control.
pairs.ml_side: Attention mechanisms in transformers (Vaswani et al. 2017): learned query-key-value attention that allows each position to attend selectively to other positions in the sequence. Multi-head attention computes multiple independent attention patterns in parallel, analogous to multiple attentional spotlight channels.
pairs.shared_idea: Transformer attention is a computational abstraction of biological attention: a context-dependent, learned weighting of inputs. The query-key comparison corresponds to matching a neural template with incoming sensory patterns. The softmax normalization corresponds to competitive inhibition. The value is the gated signal passed forward.
pairs.notes: Attention in ML was explicitly inspired by neuroscience (Bahdanau et al. cite biologically inspired attention). However, the neuroscience literature on cortical attention uses completely different formalisms. Finding early neuroscience attention papers and connecting them to transformer papers requires semantic similarity beyond vocabulary overlap.

pairs.label: Epidemic spreading models (SIR) → Graph neural network propagation
pairs.physics_or_math_side: SIR model and epidemic spreading on networks: compartmental models (Susceptible-Infected-Recovered) describing how a contagion propagates through a contact graph. Each node transitions between states based on contact with neighbors. Extensions include SIS, SEIR, heterogeneous networks. Used in public health and network science.
pairs.ml_side: Graph neural network propagation: GCN-style message passing propagates node features through the graph by averaging (or weighted-summing) neighbor features. The graph convolution operation is mathematically equivalent to one step of a linear diffusion/spreading process on the graph.
pairs.shared_idea: Graph diffusion and epidemic spreading share the same mathematical backbone: a transition matrix (normalized adjacency) applied repeatedly to a state vector. GCN is equivalent to k steps of a random walk (diffusion). The SIR model's spreading dynamics on a network and the GCN's feature aggregation both operate via neighbor summation weighted by graph structure.
pairs.notes: The mathematical equivalence between graph diffusion and GCN propagation is noted in several GNN theory papers (Klicpera et al. on APPNP). But the epidemiology literature predates GNNs by decades and has no connection to them in its citation graph.

pairs.label: Compressed sensing → Sparse autoencoders / dictionary learning
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Compressed sensing (CS): a sampling theory showing that a sparse signal can be recovered from far fewer measurements than Shannon-Nyquist theory requires, provided the measurement matrix satisfies the Restricted Isometry Property (RIP). Recovery via L1 minimization (LASSO) or greedy pursuit algorithms.
pairs.ml_side: Sparse autoencoders and dictionary learning: neural networks trained to produce sparse latent codes. Dictionary learning (Olshausen & Field 1996) learns overcomplete bases where each input is represented as a sparse combination. Sparse autoencoders (including recent interpretability-motivated ones) learn sparse features in hidden layers.
pairs.shared_idea: Both exploit sparsity as a structural prior on representations. Dictionary learning is the adaptive version of compressed sensing: instead of fixed measurement matrices, the dictionary is learned from data. L1 regularization is the common computational tool. The incoherence condition in CS corresponds to the overcompleteness and low mutual coherence of learned dictionaries.
pairs.notes: Olshausen & Field 1996 predates compressed sensing (Candes & Tao 2006) and the modern ML sparse autoencoder literature. The connection is strong but the temporal and terminological gaps make it hard to find automatically.

pairs.label: Entropy production in nonequilibrium systems → Loss landscape sharpness / flatness
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Entropy production in nonequilibrium thermodynamics: a system driven out of equilibrium continuously produces entropy. The rate of entropy production relates to the irreversibility of the dynamics and is minimized at steady states. The fluctuation theorem quantifies the asymmetry between forward and time-reversed processes.
pairs.ml_side: Sharpness and flatness of the loss landscape: flat minima generalize better than sharp ones (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber 1997, revisited by Keskar et al. 2017). SAM (Sharpness-Aware Minimization) explicitly seeks flat minima. The trace of the Hessian is a proxy for sharpness. SGD with large learning rate preferentially finds flat minima.
pairs.shared_idea: Both concern the geometry of energy/loss landscapes and the relationship between dynamics (stochastic gradient descent vs thermal fluctuations) and where in the landscape the system settles. Flat loss minima correspond to wide energy basins in the thermodynamic analogy; sharp minima correspond to deep, narrow wells. SGD's implicit regularization toward flat minima is analogous to thermal fluctuations avoiding deep narrow wells.
pairs.notes: This connection is largely metaphorical rather than mathematically precise — the mapping between thermodynamic concepts and loss landscape concepts is suggestive but not derivational. Useful for testing whether the model finds loose conceptual connections, not just tight mathematical ones.

pairs.label: Efficient market hypothesis → No-free-lunch theorems
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH): in financial markets with rational agents and freely available information, prices already reflect all available information. No trading strategy can systematically outperform the market using publicly available data. Prices follow a random walk (weak-form EMH).
pairs.ml_side: No-free-lunch (NFL) theorems (Wolpert & Macready 1997): any two optimization algorithms have the same average performance when averaged over all possible functions. Equivalently, no learning algorithm outperforms any other when evaluated over all possible data distributions. Generalizing beyond the training distribution requires prior assumptions.
pairs.shared_idea: Both say that systematic improvement is impossible without exploiting structure. EMH says prices cannot be predicted without access to information not yet in the price. NFL says no learner can generalize without a prior bias toward the true data-generating process. The connection is that both are theorems about the limits of exploiting information in the absence of structural assumptions.
pairs.notes: This pair is conceptually analogous rather than mathematically equivalent. It tests whether the model finds broad philosophical connections across fields, not just tight technical ones. Likely a hard pair for the model.

pairs.label: Power law distributions in complex systems → Heavy-tailed loss landscapes in deep learning
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Power law distributions in complex systems: many natural and social systems exhibit scale-free behavior where quantities follow a power law P(x) ~ x^(-alpha). Examples: degree distributions in social networks, earthquake magnitudes (Gutenberg-Richter law), word frequency (Zipf's law). Related to self-organized criticality.
pairs.ml_side: Heavy-tailed weight matrices in deep learning: Martin & Mahoney (2019-2021) observe that well-trained neural network weight matrices have heavy-tailed singular value distributions (power law eigenvalue spectra). This correlates with generalization quality and motivates the PowerLaw metric for model quality assessment without a test set.
pairs.shared_idea: Both describe the emergence of power law structure in systems operating near criticality. Well-trained neural networks are hypothesized to operate near the edge of chaos / criticality, and the power law weight spectra are a signature of this. The connection to self-organized criticality in complex systems is the underlying theoretical link.
pairs.notes: Martin & Mahoney's papers directly connect both sides and use physics terminology. The challenge is finding earlier power law / complex systems papers that have no connection to the ML literature.

pairs.label: Sparse Bayesian learning (Bayesian LASSO) → Pruning and lottery ticket hypothesis
pairs.physics_or_math_side: Sparse Bayesian learning (Tipping 2001): a Bayesian framework for regression and classification that places sparsity-inducing priors (e.g. Laplace, Horseshoe, Spike-and-Slab) on model parameters. The posterior concentrates weight on a small subset of features. Relevance Vector Machine is a sparse Bayesian kernel method.
pairs.ml_side: Neural network pruning and the lottery ticket hypothesis (Frankle & Carlin 2019): large neural networks contain sparse subnetworks ('winning tickets') that, when trained in isolation from scratch, match the full network's performance. Magnitude pruning, structured pruning, and Bayesian pruning are the main approaches.
pairs.shared_idea: Both seek sparse representations via prior-driven selection of which parameters matter. Sparse Bayesian learning places explicit sparsity priors; neural network pruning implicitly searches for the same structure. The lottery ticket hypothesis is, in Bayesian terms, the claim that the posterior of a trained network is concentrated on a sparse subset of parameters — which is exactly what a spike-and-slab prior would predict.
pairs.notes: Both sides are within or adjacent to cs.LG / stat.ML, so the categorical distance is small. The challenge is finding older SBL papers (Tipping 2001, Lewicki & Sejnowski 2000) that have no citation links to the modern pruning literature.
